source: Johnny's Selected Seeds succession planting guide; Cornell Cooperative Extension small-farm planting calendars; UF/IFAS Extension Florida vegetable production handbook; UC ANR Master Gardener succession planting publications; SARE Cover Crops field guide; Eliot Coleman, The New Organic Grower (succession-planting tables). Frost-anchored windows use last-spring-frost (LSF) as day 0; negative days fall before LSF and positive days after. Climate notes flag the climate types where the base window materially shifts; absence of a climate note means the base window is workable.

climateNotes.maritime: Season extends comfortably through June; keep sowing every 2 weeks until daytime highs cross 75F.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Bolts by mid-May; stop spring sowings around day +21 and switch to phase 2 early.
climateNotes.arid: Shade cloth (30 percent) extends the spring window by 2-3 weeks into early summer.

chains.notes: Choose loose-leaf and Batavian types for the most heat tolerance late in the spring window.
Heat-tolerant climbing substitute when lettuce bolts; one planting on a trellis fills the summer salad gap.

climateNotes.maritime: Carries into November under a low tunnel.
climateNotes.continental: Stop sowings 30 days before first fall frost; cold-hardy varieties (Winter Density, Rouge d'Hiver) extend by 2 weeks under row cover.

chains.notes: Fall succession; pick cold-hardy varieties as the window closes.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds succession guide; Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Romaine takes longer than leaf lettuce; space sowings 3 weeks to avoid harvest pileups.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Best fall crop; sow weekly here, the cool season is short.

chains.notes: Fall romaine often outperforms spring romaine in flavor and storage life.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UC ANR lettuce production guide

chains.notes: Butterheads bolt fast in heat; transplant 4-week starts every 2 weeks for steady heads.

climateNotes.maritime: Mid-September transplants size up under cool fall nights for late-October harvest.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds succession guide; UC ANR Master Gardener publications

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring window is narrow; stop by day +7 to avoid bolting.

chains.notes: Cold-tolerant; first sowings can go in as soon as soil works.
Heat-tolerant cooking-greens substitute; single planting after soil reaches 60F.

climateNotes.continental: Bloomsdale and Tyee overwinter under straw mulch in zone 5 and warmer.

chains.notes: Fall spinach is the best spinach; pick savoyed types for cold tolerance.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Eliot Coleman, The New Organic Grower

chains.notes: Cut at 4 inches before flower stalks elongate.

climateNotes.maritime: Holds in the ground through December under a low tunnel.

chains.notes: Summer gap is intentional; arugula goes bitter and flowers in heat above 80F.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds succession guide; Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Cut-and-come-again; harvest at 5 inches and the crown regrows twice.
Most cold-tolerant of the brassica greens; sweet after a frost.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Best fall crop; spring sowings bolt before heading.

chains.notes: Use small head varieties (Toy Choi) for tighter spacing.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds Asian greens guide; UF/IFAS Extension

chains.notes: Single spring planting carries through summer when picked weekly from the outside in.

climateNotes.maritime: Often overwinters; cut back hard in February and the same plant resumes.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall planting is the main crop; spring bolts by June.

chains.notes: Second sowing for fall and overwintering.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Eliot Coleman, The New Organic Grower

chains.notes: Spring planting; harvest from the bottom up so the crown keeps growing.

climateNotes.continental: Frost-sweetened fall kale is the prize crop; sow by mid-July for zone 5.
climateNotes.maritime: Overwinters reliably under a low tunnel.

chains.notes: Fall storage kale; lacinato holds quality longest in the ground.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UC ANR Master Gardener publications

chains.notes: Spring sowings; ready in 25-30 days. Stop when daytime highs cross 75F.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall is the main season; summer sowings split and turn woody.

chains.notes: Fall sowings size up slower and store longer.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds succession guide; Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Keep seedbed evenly moist for 14 days; spotty germination is the main carrot succession failure.

climateNotes.continental: Fall carrots store best in the ground under straw; harvest as needed through January in zone 5.
climateNotes.arid: Mulch heavily to keep soil temperature below 85F for germination.

chains.notes: Fall and storage crop.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; OSU Extension

chains.notes: Soak seedballs 8 hours before sowing for faster, more even germination.
Fall beets are the storage crop; harvest at 2-3 inches for best texture.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Penn State Extension

chains.notes: Salad turnips (Hakurei) at 40 days; storage types (Purple Top) at 60 days.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall is the main season; spring sowings bolt by May.

chains.notes: Fall and storage crop; sweetens after first light frost.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UF/IFAS Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Sow late July through August for fall harvest into December.
climateNotes.continental: Plant 10-12 weeks before first hard freeze for full-size roots.

chains.notes: Daikons need cool nights to size up; spring plantings bolt before bulbing.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds Asian roots guide; WSU Extension

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Stop sowings 70 days before first fall frost; rust pressure rises late summer.
climateNotes.maritime: Last sowing by day +70; bean season is short here.

chains.notes: Wait for soil at 60F; rotting risk is high in cold wet soil. Sow every 2-3 weeks for continuous picking.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Johnny's Selected Seeds succession guide

chains.notes: Spring peas; trellis at planting time. Plant as soon as soil works.
Pull spent pea vines, leave roots and nodules in place, replant the same bed to beans.

climateNotes.maritime: Fall peas reliable here; harvest into November.
climateNotes.continental: Fall peas only succeed where first frost is after October 15.

chains.notes: Fall pea on the same trellis; reuses the structure twice.

entries.source: Eliot Coleman, The New Organic Grower; Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Two or three sowings 2 weeks apart spread harvest; all-at-once pods are the default failure.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds edamame guide; Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Spring sowings; first crop is the heaviest.

climateNotes.maritime: Fall window is reliable.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall is the main season; spring is too brief.

chains.notes: Fall sowings for cool-season harvest.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UC ANR Master Gardener publications

chains.notes: Spring crop; transplant 4-6 week starts. Side shoots extend harvest 4-6 weeks past the main head.

climateNotes.continental: Fall broccoli is the better crop in zones 5-6; cool nights tighten heads.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall sowings only; spring is too short.

chains.notes: Fall direct sow when soil cools below 75F.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Penn State Extension

chains.notes: Early-season variety (Early Jersey Wakefield, Caraflex) for late spring harvest.
Mid-season variety; transplant when daytime highs drop below 80F.

climateNotes.maritime: Storage savoy holds through December.
climateNotes.continental: Plant 12 weeks before first hard freeze for storage size.

chains.notes: Storage variety (savoy or red drumhead); frost-sweetened.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; OSU Extension

chains.notes: Spring crop; cauliflower bolts at heat above 80F, so timing is tight.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall is the only reliable season; spring almost always buttons.

chains.notes: Fall crop; the better cauliflower season in most climates.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UC ANR Master Gardener publications

chains.notes: First sowing once soil is 65F. Trellis to extend bearing life.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Second planting outruns the first against downy mildew which arrives in late July.
climateNotes.maritime: Second sowing only succeeds in long-season pockets; usually skip.

chains.notes: Second planting takes over as the first declines from bacterial wilt or downy mildew. 3-4 weeks apart.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UF/IFAS Extension

chains.notes: First planting; squash vine borer typically takes plants by late July.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Second planting is essential against vine borer; first plants rarely make August.
climateNotes.maritime: Second planting often succeeds well into September.

chains.notes: Replacement planting around the time borers hit phase 1.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Penn State Extension

chains.notes: Crookneck and patty-pan together cover the early summer window.
Second planting carries through August into early fall.

entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension; Texas A&M AgriLife Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Last sowing by day +60; basil sulks below 55F nights.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Downy mildew arrives mid-summer; later sowings outrun first-planting losses.

chains.notes: Pinch flower buds; second and third plantings step in as the first goes woody.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Johnny's Selected Seeds basil guide

chains.notes: Bolts fast in long days and heat; 2-week succession is the only way to keep leaves coming.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall is the main season; cilantro is a cool-season herb here.
climateNotes.arid: Use Calypso or Slow Bolt strains; even fall sowings bolt fast in low-humidity heat.

chains.notes: Fall succession; cool nights slow bolting.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds cilantro guide; UC ANR Master Gardener publications

chains.notes: For leaf dill (fronds), succession every 3 weeks; for seed dill, single early planting that flowers in midsummer.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Johnny's Selected Seeds herb guide

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Powdery mildew arrives in August; later sowings hold longer in bloom.

chains.notes: Two or three sowings keeps cut-flower production steady from July through frost. Pinch first stems at 8 inches to encourage branching.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds cut flower guide; Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Early planting blooms July through August.
Mid-season planting blooms September through frost; sulphureus types are more heat-tolerant.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds cut flower guide; Xerces Society pollinator publications

climateNotes.continental: Last sowing by day +60 to bloom before first frost.
climateNotes.maritime: Late sowings struggle to ripen; stop by day +49.

chains.notes: Branching types (Autumn Beauty, Lemon Queen) flower continuously off a single plant; single-stem types (ProCut, Sunrich) need 2-week succession for continuous blooms.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds cut flower guide; UC ANR Master Gardener publications

chains.notes: Fall-sown the previous September; overwinters and fixes 70-130 lb N per acre by spring termination.
Roll-crimp the clover at full bloom 2 weeks before transplanting; tomatoes go through the mulch into stored N.

climateNotes.continental: Sow by day +160 for adequate fall establishment in zones 5-6.

chains.notes: After tomato harvest, pull plants and overseed winter rye for next year's biomass and erosion control.

entries.source: SARE Cover Crops field guide; Rodale Institute farming systems trial

chains.notes: Fast warm-season smother crop; flowers at 5-6 weeks. Mow at first bloom before seed sets.
Fall brassica transplanted into the rolled buckwheat residue; weed-free bed and freed-up phosphorus.

climateNotes.continental: Sow by day +150 in zones 5-6; vetch needs 6 weeks of growth before hard freeze.

chains.notes: Overseeded between brassica rows in fall; overwinters and fixes 100-150 lb N per acre by spring.

entries.source: SARE Cover Crops field guide; Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Fall-sown legume cover; overwinters in zones 6 and warmer, fixes 90-150 lb N per acre by spring termination.
Heavy nitrogen feeder placed after the winter pea; takes up the freed-up N over the season.

climateNotes.maritime: Frost-seed in February instead, the cool wet spring establishes the clover well.
climateNotes.continental: Overseed at last cultivation around day +70 in zones 5-6 for fall establishment.

chains.notes: Overseeded into the corn between rows at last cultivation; provides ground cover after corn harvest and overwinters for next year.

entries.source: SARE Cover Crops field guide; Penn State Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Cool spring extends head development; expect main head 60-70 days from transplant.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Plant transplants by day -14 to escape mid-spring heat that triggers premature bolting.

chains.notes: Single main head harvest 60-75 days after transplant; cut at full bud size before flowering.

climateNotes.maritime: Side shoots continue producing weekly into October; nitrogen side-dress at main head harvest extends production.
climateNotes.continental: Side shoots harvested for 4-6 weeks after main head; smaller florets but continuous yield.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Side shoots fade by July as heat accumulates; pull plants and rotate to summer crop.

chains.notes: Same plants from phase 1; harvest side shoots weekly. The classic single-planting continuous harvest.

climateNotes.maritime: Fall plantings produce best in the maritime climate; main heads form during cool September weather.
climateNotes.continental: Set transplants 90 days before first fall frost; mulch heavily as cold sets in.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Set transplants in August once nights cool; main head fills during the long fall season.

chains.notes: Fall succession plantings produce main heads then side shoots into winter under row cover.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Johnny's Selected Seeds succession guide

climateNotes.maritime: Early cabbage cultivars (Early Jersey Wakefield, Stonehead) mature 60-70 days; spring harvest before heat.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Set transplants in February or earlier; harvest by late April before heat bolts plants.

chains.notes: Early-cabbage phase: small fast-maturing cultivars for fresh use in May-June.

climateNotes.continental: Mid-cabbage cultivars (Copenhagen Market, Golden Acre) fit between spring and fall plantings.
climateNotes.maritime: Mid-cabbage rarely needed in mild maritime; spring plantings carry through summer.

chains.notes: Mid-season cabbage for July-August harvest; bridges spring and fall.

climateNotes.continental: Late storage cultivars (January King, Brunswick) need 90-100 days for proper head formation; transplant by July 1.
climateNotes.maritime: Storage cabbage extends through fall; January King hand-picks in December under low tunnel.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall transplants set in August once nights cool; harvest December-January.

chains.notes: Storage cabbage phase: dense long-storage cultivars for winter use. Harvest after first hard frost for best storage.

entries.source: Coleman, The New Organic Grower; Johnny's Selected Seeds succession guide

climateNotes.maritime: Plant sets in March on raised beds; harvest green onions through May.
climateNotes.continental: Plant sets as soon as soil works; harvest green onions through June.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Plant sets in November-December for green onion harvest in late winter.

chains.notes: Onion sets phase: small bulbs planted for fresh green onion harvest. Quick first phase before main crop sown.

climateNotes.continental: Transplants from late February starts yield mature bulbs in August-September.
climateNotes.maritime: Transplants fit the long cool growing window for storage onions; plant in April.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Transplants set in January-February for May-June bulb harvest.

chains.notes: Main storage crop from transplants; ready for fresh kitchen use in summer and cure for storage.

Scallion succession phase: dedicated green-onion strips for continuous fresh harvest behind the main crop. Direct seed scallion strips every 3 weeks through the growing season for continuous green-onion harvest.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Coleman, The New Organic Grower

climateNotes.maritime: Plant first earlies in March; harvest June-July as small new potatoes.
climateNotes.continental: Wait until soil reaches 50F before planting; first earlies mature in 60-75 days.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Plant first earlies in January-February; harvest by late April before summer heat.

chains.notes: Early-potato phase: 60-75 day red-skinned new potato cultivars (Red Norland, Yukon Gold, Caribe). Fresh use only; no long storage.

climateNotes.continental: Mid-season cultivars planted in late April; mature in 90-100 days for August harvest.
climateNotes.maritime: Mid-season planted in April; harvest August-September.

chains.notes: Mid-season potato phase: 90-100 day cultivars. Bridge between fresh new potatoes and storage crops.

climateNotes.continental: Late storage cultivars (Kennebec, Katahdin, Burbank) planted in May; harvest September-October.
climateNotes.maritime: Late cultivars carry through October harvest; cool soil at planting time favors emergence.

chains.notes: Storage potato phase: 110-130 day cultivars for winter storage. Cure 2 weeks at 60F before cold storage.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Penn State Extension potato production

climateNotes.maritime: Sow first block when soil reaches 60F; continue every 2 weeks through June.
climateNotes.continental: Sow first block around last frost in deep loam soils; continue every 2 weeks through July 1.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Sow continuous blocks every 2 weeks from late February through May, then again August-September for fall crop.
climateNotes.arid: Heat-tolerant cultivars (Mirai, Trinity) handle summer plantings; shade cloth during tasseling preserves pollination.

chains.notes: Two-week stagger gives 8-10 days of fresh corn per planting block. Sow blocks of 4x10 ft for adequate pollination; small rows have poor kernel set.

entries.source: Johnny's Selected Seeds succession guide; Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Plant snap peas in February-March for May-June harvest; cool maritime climate extends harvest into July.
climateNotes.continental: Plant snap peas as soon as soil works; harvest June.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Plant in November-December; harvest February-March.

chains.notes: Spring snap pea phase: bush or short-vine types fit succession bed. Harvest until heat triggers powdery mildew and pod toughening.

climateNotes.continental: Direct sow bush beans immediately after pea harvest; pods ready in 50-60 days.
climateNotes.maritime: Wait for soil to reach 60F (often late May) before direct seeding bush beans.

chains.notes: Summer bush-bean phase: fills the heat-of-summer window between cool-season legume crops.

climateNotes.maritime: Fall snap peas thrive in the cool maritime climate; harvest October-November.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall peas direct-sown in September for November harvest.
climateNotes.continental: Fall peas need 60-70 day window; sow by August 1 in zone 5; harvest before first hard freeze.

chains.notes: Fall snap-pea phase: cool-season legume returns after summer heat. Cold-hardy cultivars extend harvest under low tunnel.

entries.source: Coleman, The New Organic Grower; Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Continuous spring sowings through April; bulbs ready in 50-60 days.
climateNotes.continental: Sow every 2 weeks from soil-workable through May 1; bulbs ready in 55-65 days.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring kohlrabi short window: sow February-March only; bolts in summer heat.

chains.notes: Spring kohlrabi phase: harvest bulbs at 2-3 inches before woody texture sets in.

climateNotes.maritime: Fall kohlrabi extends through November; carries over winter under low tunnel.
climateNotes.continental: Fall sowings from August through early September; harvest before hard freeze.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall is the primary kohlrabi season; sow September-November.

chains.notes: Fall kohlrabi phase: cool-season return for storage and fresh use.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Coleman, The New Organic Grower

climateNotes.maritime: Spring bok choy from March direct sowings; cool maritime spring extends to early June.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring window narrow: sow February-March only; bolts quickly in heat.

chains.notes: Spring bok choy phase: baby cultivars (Shanghai) fit short window; bolt-resistant types extend the window.

climateNotes.maritime: Fall bok choy through December under row cover.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Primary bok choy season; continuous sowings September through November.

chains.notes: Fall bok choy phase: bolt-resistant varieties favor the long cool fall window.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; OSU Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Spring transplants for June harvest; cool spring extends the harvest window.
climateNotes.continental: Wait until last frost for transplants; mature in 65-75 days.

chains.notes: Spring napa phase: tight short window before heat bolts plants. Fast-bolt-resistant cultivars (Bilko, Minuet) preferred.

climateNotes.maritime: Fall napa through November; cool autumn favors heading.
climateNotes.continental: Set transplants in early August for October harvest; cold-hardy through 25F.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Primary napa season; set transplants August-September for fall heading.

chains.notes: Fall napa phase: dominant napa season; long cool fall favors tight heads.

entries.source: OSU Extension Asian greens; Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Spring kale from March transplants; harvest baby leaves from May, full leaves through summer.
climateNotes.continental: Spring transplants harvested through summer.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring window short; harvest by early June before heat slows growth.

chains.notes: Spring kale phase: continuous cut-and-come harvest from May through summer; remove flower spikes to extend harvest.

climateNotes.maritime: Fall kale extends through January under low tunnel; sweetens after frost.
climateNotes.continental: Fall transplants in August produce best leaves in October-November; some overwinter under row cover.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall is the primary kale season; transplants from August through October.

chains.notes: Fall kale phase: cold-hardy cultivars (Winterbor, Redbor, Toscano) sweeten with frost and extend the harvest into winter.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Coleman, The New Organic Grower

climateNotes.maritime: Spring mustard direct-sown through May; ready as baby greens in 21 days.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring window short: February-March only.

chains.notes: Spring mustard phase: continuous sowings for baby cut greens.

climateNotes.maritime: Fall mustard extends through December under row cover.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Primary mustard season; September-November.

chains.notes: Fall mustard phase: cool-season return for fresh and storage.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UF/IFAS Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Single spring planting carries through fall; chard tolerates maritime summer better than most greens.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring transplants in February; harvest through June.
climateNotes.continental: Spring transplants in May; continuous cut harvest June through October.

chains.notes: Single planting phase: cut-and-come-again harvest from June through October. One transplanting supports a full season of leaf harvest.

climateNotes.maritime: Fall chard through December under row cover.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall chard transplant in August; harvest through January.

chains.notes: Fall chard phase (optional): for fall and winter harvest; replaces or extends summer planting.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Coleman, The New Organic Grower

climateNotes.maritime: Spring fennel from transplants for June bulb harvest; cool maritime climate extends bulb fill.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring window very short: February transplants only; bolts in heat.

chains.notes: Spring fennel phase: bolt-resistant Florence-type bulbs for fresh use.

climateNotes.maritime: Fall fennel through November; cool autumn favors bulb development.
climateNotes.continental: Fall transplants in August for October harvest.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Primary fennel season; transplants from August through October.

chains.notes: Fall fennel phase: cool autumn produces the highest-quality bulbs.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Cool maritime spring extends cilantro harvest through May; sow every 3 weeks.
climateNotes.continental: Spring cilantro from March direct sowings; ready in 30 days as baby herb.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Sow February-March; finish harvest by April before heat.

chains.notes: Spring cilantro phase: continuous sowings every 3 weeks until heat triggers bolt. Slow-bolt cultivars (Calypso, Santo) extend the window.
Coriander seed phase: bolted spring plants produce harvestable coriander seed for kitchen use. Allow spring plants to bolt and set seed; harvest coriander seed when seed pods dry on plant.

climateNotes.maritime: Fall cilantro through November; cool autumn extends harvest.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Primary cilantro season; continuous sowings September-November.

chains.notes: Fall cilantro phase: cool-season return for fresh herb harvest into winter.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Coleman, The New Organic Grower

climateNotes.maritime: Spring transplants extend through fall; parsley tolerates maritime summer.
climateNotes.continental: Spring transplants harvested June through October.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring transplants in February for harvest through May.

chains.notes: Spring parsley phase: single transplanting supports continuous cut-and-come harvest through summer.

climateNotes.maritime: Fall parsley through December under row cover; biennial habit may overwinter.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall transplants extend harvest through January.

chains.notes: Fall parsley phase: cool-season return; some plants overwinter for spring resumption of harvest before flowering.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Spring tatsoi through May; harvest at rosette stage before heat triggers bolt.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring window short: February-March.

chains.notes: Spring tatsoi phase: continuous sowings for spoon-leaf rosette harvest.

climateNotes.maritime: Fall tatsoi through January under low tunnel; cold-hardy.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Primary tatsoi season; sowings September-November.

chains.notes: Fall tatsoi phase: very cold-hardy; carries to 15F under row cover.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Spring mizuna through May; harvest as baby greens at 21 days.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring window short.

chains.notes: Spring mizuna phase: continuous baby-leaf cut harvest.

climateNotes.maritime: Fall mizuna through December under row cover.

chains.notes: Fall mizuna phase: continuous baby-leaf harvest from September into winter.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.continental: Wait until soil temperature reaches 70F before transplanting; black plastic mulch helps warm soil.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Plant by early May for August harvest of early cultivars; second planting in June for September fruit.
climateNotes.arid: Heat-loving crop; drip irrigation supports vines through summer.

chains.notes: Single planting phase: watermelon vines are long-season crops that occupy bed space for 75-100 days. Most beds get one watermelon planting per season.

entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension watermelon; Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.continental: Plant in late May when soil warms; harvest 70-90 days from transplant.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Plant in April for July harvest; second planting in June for September.
climateNotes.arid: Drip irrigation supports cantaloupe through hot dry summers.

chains.notes: Single planting phase: cantaloupe vines occupy bed for 80-100 days. Trellising preserves bed space in small gardens.

entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension melon production

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Primary okra season; direct sow in April for July-October harvest.
climateNotes.continental: Wait for soil at 65F; northern okra production limited to 90-day window.

chains.notes: Single planting phase: okra produces continuously from first pod through frost. Daily harvest required for tender pods.

entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Cool maritime nights slow eggplant production; row cover or low tunnel maintains warmth.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Transplants in April for July-October harvest.
climateNotes.arid: Heat tolerant; produces through extreme summer heat with consistent irrigation.

chains.notes: Single planting phase: eggplant produces continuously from July through frost. Pick at 4-6 inches for tender fruit.

entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Cool nights slow pepper production; row cover extends warmth.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Transplants in April for July-October fresh harvest; second cool-season harvest possible in fall.
climateNotes.arid: Shade cloth during midsummer heat preserves fruit set; pepper plants tolerant of heat.

chains.notes: Single planting phase: pepper plants produce continuously from July through frost. Pick at full size and color stage for highest quality.

entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Choose early-determinate cultivars (Stupice, Glacier) for short maritime summers.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring transplants in March for June-August harvest; fall transplants in August for October harvest.
climateNotes.continental: Transplant after last frost; harvest through August-September.
climateNotes.arid: Heat-set cultivars (Heatmaster, Solar Set) tolerate extreme summer heat.

chains.notes: Single planting phase: indeterminate vines produce continuously through frost. Determinate varieties produce in a single concentrated harvest window.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall tomato season: set transplants in August for October-November harvest.

chains.notes: Fall tomato phase (warm climates only): transplants for fall harvest in regions with mild winters.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UF/IFAS Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Plant garlic October-November; harvest July.
climateNotes.continental: Plant 3-4 weeks before ground freezes; harvest late July.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Plant November-December; harvest April-May.

chains.notes: Fall garlic plant phase: cloves overwinter in soil; harvest after scapes form and leaves brown the next summer.
Companion shallot phase: planted simultaneously with garlic for parallel harvest. Plant shallot bulbs at the same time as garlic; harvest concurrently.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Cool maritime climate favors celery; harvest July-September.
climateNotes.continental: Spring transplants for July-August harvest.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring transplants in February for May harvest; primary fall season.

chains.notes: Spring celery phase: requires consistent moisture and rich soil; bolts in summer heat.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Primary celery season; fall transplants in August for November-December harvest.
climateNotes.maritime: Fall celery extends harvest through October.

chains.notes: Fall celery phase (warm climates): produces highest-quality celery in cool autumn conditions.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UF/IFAS Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Cool maritime climate ideal for leeks; harvest through January under mulch.
climateNotes.continental: Spring transplants for fall-winter harvest; cold-hardy.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall transplant primary season; spring planting bolts in heat.

chains.notes: Single planting phase: leeks need 90-150 days from transplant; hill soil around stems for blanched white shanks.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Fall daikon for storage; harvest after first hard frost.
climateNotes.continental: Direct sow 50-60 days before first fall frost.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Primary daikon season; sow September-October.

chains.notes: Single fall planting phase: daikon roots size up best in cool autumn weather; cellar-store after harvest.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Fall rutabaga for storage; cool autumn perfect for root development.
climateNotes.continental: Sow 90-100 days before first hard freeze.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Sow August-September for November harvest.

chains.notes: Single fall planting phase: rutabaga sweetens with frost; harvest and store in cool conditions.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Transplants in June for November-December harvest; sweetens with frost.
climateNotes.continental: Set transplants by July 1 for October-November harvest.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall transplant from late July for winter harvest.

chains.notes: Single planting phase: brussels sprouts need 90-120 days from transplant. Top plants 3-4 weeks before harvest to even sprout size.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Fall cauliflower for October-November harvest; cool autumn favors tight heads.
climateNotes.continental: Set transplants in July for September-October harvest.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Primary cauliflower season; transplant in August for November-December harvest.

chains.notes: Single fall planting phase: cauliflower needs 60-90 days from transplant; cool autumn produces best curds. Self-blanching cultivars simplify management.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Continuous transplants every 3 weeks supply fresh basil through summer.
climateNotes.continental: Every 3 weeks from late May through July for continuous harvest.
climateNotes.arid: Heat-tolerant; consistent irrigation supports plant vigor.

chains.notes: Genovese basil 3-week succession phase: continuous fresh-pesto cut harvest. Mature plants get woody after 6-8 weeks; new transplants maintain quality.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Johnny's Selected Seeds

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Heat-tolerant Thai basil thrives through southern summers.
climateNotes.continental: Every 4 weeks from late May through August.

chains.notes: Thai basil succession phase: continuous harvest with heat tolerance. Stronger aroma than Genovese; preferred for Asian cooking.

entries.source: UF/IFAS Extension

climateNotes.continental: Continuous sowings every 3 weeks for fresh leaf and seed harvest.
climateNotes.maritime: Cool maritime climate extends harvest through fall; sowings through August.

chains.notes: Continuous dill succession phase: sow every 3 weeks for tender leaves; allow some plants to bolt for seed.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Summer savory succession phase: continuous fresh leaf harvest through summer. Continuous summer sowings; heat-loving annual herb pairs with bean dishes.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Plant tubers after last frost; bloom from late July through October.
climateNotes.continental: Plant tubers when soil reaches 60F; bloom August through hard frost.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Plant tubers in March-April; bloom through summer and fall.

chains.notes: Single tuber-planting phase: dahlia tubers planted in spring bloom from midsummer through frost. Continuous cut-flower harvest from one planting; deadhead spent flowers to extend bloom.

entries.source: USDA-NRCS pollinator garden; Floret Farm cut flower guide

climateNotes.maritime: Sweet peas thrive in cool maritime climate; sow in February for May-July bloom.
climateNotes.continental: Sow at last frost or earlier; bloom in June.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Plant in November-December for February-April bloom; spring window is the primary sweet pea season.

chains.notes: Spring sweet pea phase: cool-season annual; trellis on a fence. Harvest deeply every other day to extend bloom.

climateNotes.maritime: Fall sweet peas through November bloom.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall sowings extend the sweet pea season for spring bloom.

chains.notes: Fall sweet pea phase (mild climates): cool-season return for fall and spring bloom.

entries.source: Floret Farm cut flower guide; USDA-NRCS

climateNotes.continental: Direct sow every 2 weeks from last frost through July for continuous cut flower production.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Continuous sowings through August; heat-loving annual.
climateNotes.arid: Heat-tolerant; thrives in arid summers with drip irrigation.

chains.notes: Continuous sunflower phase: 2-week stagger supplies fresh cut flowers throughout summer. Single-stem cultivars (ProCut, Soraya) for cut-flower trade; branching cultivars for bouquet additions.

entries.source: Floret Farm cut flower guide; Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Cool maritime spring extends snapdragon bloom; harvest June-August.
climateNotes.continental: Spring transplants for June-July bloom; spike production benefits from cool nights.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall is the primary snapdragon season; transplants from October for spring bloom.

chains.notes: Spring snapdragon phase: tall single-stem cultivars (Rocket, Madame Butterfly) for cut flower; spike harvest at one-third bloom.

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall transplants extend bloom through winter for southern cut-flower production.
climateNotes.maritime: Fall snapdragon through December.

chains.notes: Fall snapdragon phase (mild climates): extended bloom from fall transplants in regions with mild winters.

entries.source: Floret Farm cut flower guide; USDA-NRCS

climateNotes.maritime: Fall sowings in September-October for spring bloom; cold treatment required for germination.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall direct sow in October for March-April bloom.
climateNotes.continental: Fall sow in October; cold winter triggers spring germination.

chains.notes: Single fall-sowing phase: larkspur requires winter cold for germination; fall direct sow yields spring cut flowers. Spring sowings typically fail to bloom.

entries.source: Floret Farm cut flower guide

climateNotes.continental: Transplants every 4 weeks from last frost through July for continuous bloom.
climateNotes.maritime: Transplants from April through August.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring transplants in February; fall transplants in September.

chains.notes: Scabiosa succession phase: continuous transplants supply pollinator-attracting cut flowers; long bloom window with deadheading.

entries.source: Floret Farm cut flower guide; Xerces Society

climateNotes.continental: Single transplant after last frost; bloom from July through October.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring transplants for summer-fall bloom; continuous deadheading extends production.
climateNotes.arid: Heat tolerant; native species thrive in arid summers.

chains.notes: Single planting phase: black-eyed Susan blooms from July through frost. Native perennial; one planting produces for multiple seasons in suitable climates.

entries.source: Floret Farm cut flower guide; USDA-NRCS

climateNotes.continental: Continuous direct sowings every 3 weeks; bloom in 5-6 weeks.
climateNotes.maritime: Sow March through August for continuous bloom.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring window: February through April.

chains.notes: Phacelia succession phase: pollinator-attracting blue lacy flowers; mass plantings draw bumblebees and parasitic wasps.

entries.source: Xerces Society; USDA-NRCS

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Amaranth thrives in southern heat; transplants in April for July-October bloom.
climateNotes.continental: Transplants after last frost; bloom August through frost.

chains.notes: Single planting phase: ornamental amaranth (Hopi Red Dye, Hot Biscuits) blooms continuously through summer and fall; heat-loving annual.

entries.source: Floret Farm cut flower guide; Native Seeds/SEARCH

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Mexican sunflower thrives in heat; transplants in April for July-October bloom.
climateNotes.continental: Transplants after last frost; reaches 6 ft height; bloom August through frost.

chains.notes: Single planting phase: tithonia is a tall heat-loving annual; pollinator-attracting cut flowers with continuous bloom.

entries.source: Xerces Society; USDA-NRCS

climateNotes.continental: Spring oats sown 2-3 weeks before last frost; mow at heading for 4-6 tons biomass.
climateNotes.maritime: Spring oats from February sowings; mow at boot stage.

chains.notes: Spring oats phase: fast cool-season cover crop; suppresses spring weeds and builds biomass.

climateNotes.continental: Buckwheat after oat termination; 4-6 week cycle from sowing to mow.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Buckwheat sown after oats; fast summer biomass.

chains.notes: Summer buckwheat phase: fills the warm-season gap with fast biomass and pollinator habitat. Mow at first bloom for highest biomass.

climateNotes.continental: Winter rye sown by October 1 for full establishment before freeze.
climateNotes.maritime: Winter rye through November sowings.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Winter rye sown in November; vigorous spring growth.

chains.notes: Winter rye phase: overwintering cover crop scavenges N and suppresses winter weeds. Roll-kill in spring for no-till mulch.

entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; SARE Cover Crops field guide

climateNotes.continental: Crimson clover sown in September; overwinters under mulch.
climateNotes.maritime: September sowings establish for spring N supply.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Crimson clover overwinters reliably; fall sowings for spring N transfer.

chains.notes: Fall crimson clover phase: prior-year cover crop establishes for spring N fixation. Mow at first bloom and roll-kill in place before transplant.

climateNotes.continental: Transplant tomato into roll-killed clover mulch; reduced fertilizer needs from clover N.
climateNotes.maritime: Tomato transplant into clover mulch; benefits from moisture retention.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring tomato transplant into clover mulch.

chains.notes: Summer tomato phase: warm-season crop benefits from N fixed by clover and mulch suppression of weeds.

climateNotes.continental: Hairy vetch sown at tomato harvest end; overwinters for next-season N supply.
climateNotes.maritime: Hairy vetch through November sowings.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Hairy vetch overwinters reliably; sow in October.

chains.notes: Hairy vetch phase: winter N-fixing cover crop after summer crop; rolls down for next-year mulch.

entries.source: USDA-ARS rolled cover crop research; SARE Cover Crops field guide

climateNotes.continental: Field pea and oats co-seeded in early spring; oats nurses pea establishment.
climateNotes.maritime: Spring pea-oat mix from February sowings.

chains.notes: Spring pea-oats phase: co-seeded for N fixation plus biomass. Mow at pea bloom for highest biomass.
Summer buckwheat phase: fast warm-season cover; pollinator habitat. Buckwheat fills summer gap; fast biomass and pollinator habitat.
Winter rye phase: scavenges residual N from prior cover crops; roll-kill in spring for no-till mulch.

entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; Penn State Extension

climateNotes.continental: Hairy vetch sown in September; overwinters for spring N supply.
climateNotes.maritime: September-October sowings.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Hairy vetch sown in October; reliable overwintering.

chains.notes: Fall vetch phase: prior-year cover crop establishes for spring N supply to corn.

climateNotes.continental: Corn no-till planted into roll-killed vetch mulch; reduced N fertilizer needs.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring corn into vetch mulch.

chains.notes: Summer corn phase: heavy nitrogen feeder benefits from vetch N supply. Roll-kill vetch at bloom for no-till mulch.
Winter rye phase: scavenges residual N from corn season; roll-kill in spring for next-year mulch.

entries.source: USDA-ARS rolled cover crop research

climateNotes.continental: Winter rye sown in September-October for full establishment.

chains.notes: Fall rye phase: prior-year overwintering cover crop scavenges N and suppresses weeds.

climateNotes.continental: Soybean no-till into roll-killed rye mulch.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Spring soybean planting after rye termination.

chains.notes: Summer soybean phase: legume contribution returns N to soil after rye scavenge.
Next-year rye phase: scavenges N from soybean residue; sets up the next corn rotation.

entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; Iowa State Extension

climateNotes.continental: Sorghum-sudan after last frost for summer biomass.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Primary summer cover crop in southern US; heavy biomass.

chains.notes: Summer sorghum-sudan phase: warm-season grass for heavy biomass and soil-building.
Summer cowpea companion phase: N fixation pairs with sorghum-sudan biomass. Co-seed cowpea with sorghum-sudan for the N-fixing companion.
Winter rye phase: overwintering cover crop scavenges N from summer cover residue.

entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; SARE Cover Crops field guide

chains.notes: Summer buckwheat phase: fills bed gap between spring and fall plantings. Buckwheat for fast 6-week summer biomass and pollinator habitat.

climateNotes.continental: Mustard cover after buckwheat mow-down; biofumigation effects after incorporation.
climateNotes.maritime: Mustard cover establishes through fall.

chains.notes: Fall mustard cover phase: biofumigant for nematode and soilborne disease suppression.

entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; SARE Cover Crops field guide

climateNotes.continental: Daikon cover crop for soil-busting biological subsoiling.
climateNotes.maritime: Daikon establishes through fall; deep taproot opens compacted soil.

chains.notes: Daikon cover phase: deep tap-root opens compacted subsoil; winter-kills in cold zones leaving channels.
Mustard cover companion phase: biofumigant effects after winter kill or incorporation. Co-seed mustard with daikon for combined biofumigation and soil-busting.

entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; SARE Cover Crops field guide

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Sunn hemp tolerates heat; fixes 50-150 lb N/acre in 8-12 weeks.

chains.notes: Summer sunn hemp phase: heat-loving N-fixer; suppresses nematodes when incorporated.

entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; UF/IFAS Extension

climateNotes.continental: Phacelia for spring pollinator habitat plus biomass; 6-week cycle.
climateNotes.maritime: Phacelia establishes through cool spring; pollinator beacon for fruit crops.

chains.notes: Spring phacelia phase: fast-cycle cover crop for pollinator habitat between cash crops.

entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart; Xerces Society

climateNotes.mediterranean: Self-seeding annual legume; fall sowings establish for spring N fixation.
climateNotes.maritime: Mild winter establishment; reseeds annually under suitable management.

chains.notes: Fall subterranean clover phase: self-seeding annual cover; persists through annual cycles in Mediterranean and maritime climates.

entries.source: USDA-NRCS; UC ANR

chains.notes: Spring lacy-phacelia phase: extended-bloom cultivar for pollinator habitat between cash crops. Lacy phacelia for spring pollinator habitat and beneficial-insect support.

entries.source: Xerces Society

climateNotes.continental: Spring berseem clover with oats nurse; 6-8 week cycle.
climateNotes.maritime: Spring berseem from March sowings.

chains.notes: Spring berseem clover phase: dryland N-fixing legume; pairs well with oats.

entries.source: USDA-NRCS Cover Crop Chart

climateNotes.maritime: Spring transplants for first-year establishment; harvest second year.
climateNotes.continental: Spring transplants; remove first-year flowers for stronger plants.

chains.notes: Establishment year phase: first-year strawberry transplants; remove blossoms for plant strength.
Production year phase: full berry harvest from established plants; renovate after harvest by cutting plants to 1 inch. Second-year mature plants produce full crop in May-June.

Renovation phase: post-harvest mowing and fertilization preserves the matted-row stand for the next year's crop. Cycles for 3-4 years before replanting. Renovation phase: mow plants, fertilize, thin runners to maintain stand density.

entries.source: OSU Extension; Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Floricane-fruiting summer raspberries (Boyne, Latham) harvest June-July.
climateNotes.continental: Summer-bearing harvest in late June through July.

chains.notes: Summer-bearing floricane phase: floricane (second-year cane) production. Prune out floricanes after fruiting.

climateNotes.maritime: Primocane-fruiting fall raspberries (Heritage, Caroline) harvest August-October.
climateNotes.continental: Fall-bearing primocane harvest September-October.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Fall primocane raspberry extends harvest through November.

chains.notes: Fall-bearing primocane phase: first-year cane production extends raspberry season. Mow all canes to ground in winter for primocane-only production.

entries.source: OSU Extension berry crops; Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.continental: Duke harvests early in the season; pair with mid and late cultivars.
climateNotes.maritime: Duke is the early northern highbush cultivar; harvest late June through early July.

chains.notes: Early blueberry phase: Duke and other early-season cultivars (Bluetta, Northland) start the harvest.
Mid-season blueberry phase: Bluecrop, Patriot, Jersey extend the harvest. Bluecrop is the standard mid-season cultivar; harvest mid-July through August.

Late blueberry phase: Elliott extends harvest into fall; pair with early and mid cultivars for 8-10 week harvest window. Elliott is the late northern highbush cultivar; harvest late August through September.

entries.source: OSU Extension berry crops; Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Cool maritime climate suits everbearing strawberries; harvest through October.
climateNotes.continental: Spring transplants for June and September harvests.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Day-neutral cultivars produce best in spring and fall windows.

chains.notes: Single planting phase: everbearing cultivars (Albion, Seascape, Tristar) fruit in spring and fall flushes from one planting.

entries.source: OSU Extension berry crops

climateNotes.maritime: Cool maritime climate ideal for rhubarb; harvest April through July.
climateNotes.continental: Crown divisions for spring harvest; perennial that produces for 10+ years.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Marginal in hot southern climates; choose shaded site.

chains.notes: Single planting phase: rhubarb is a long-lived perennial; one planting harvested every spring for 10+ years. Do not harvest first year.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.maritime: Spring crown plantings establish for harvest 3 years later; perennial 15+ years.
climateNotes.continental: Asparagus is the longest-lived vegetable perennial; spring crown plantings.
climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Mild winters favor early spring spear production; harvest March-April.

chains.notes: Single establishment phase: asparagus crowns establish the bed for 15-20 years of spring spear harvest. Wait 2-3 years for first full harvest.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; OSU Extension

chains.notes: Single planting phase: spearmint perennial; one planting in containers produces continuous fresh herb for years. Mint is a long-lived perennial; one planting produces for decades. Contain spread.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Spring oregano phase: perennial herb produces continuously through summer and into fall. Cut back hard at season end. Oregano is a perennial herb; one planting produces for 5+ years. Divide every 3-4 years for continued vigor.
Thyme companion phase: perennial herb co-planted with oregano in a shared bed for kitchen-garden harvests.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Single planting phase: chive perennial clump; divide every 3-4 years for renewed vigor. Chive perennial clump; one planting produces continuous spring through fall harvest for 5+ years.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Single planting phase: lemon balm perennial herb; continuous harvest with deadheading to prevent self-seeding. Lemon balm spreads aggressively by rhizome; contain in a bed corner or in a buried container.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

chains.notes: Single planting phase: sage perennial; replace every 4-5 years as plants become woody and unproductive. Sage perennial herb; one planting produces for 4-5 years before woody renewal.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Lemon verbena is a tender perennial in zones 9+; overwinters in southern gardens.
climateNotes.continental: Grow in pots and overwinter indoors in cold zones.

chains.notes: Spring lemon verbena phase: tender perennial in warm climates; treat as annual or pot for overwintering in cold zones.

entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension

climateNotes.humid_subtropical: Tulsi (holy basil) thrives in southern heat; primary medicinal-herb season.
climateNotes.continental: Tender annual in cold zones; transplant after last frost.
climateNotes.arid: Heat-tolerant; produces continuously through hot summers.

chains.notes: Single planting phase: Rama tulsi (Ocimum sanctum) for medicinal leaf harvest. Pinch flowering tips to extend leaf production.

entries.source: ICAR-Indian Institute of Spices Research
